active_paradigms.idea: Custom Triton kernel fusing dP computation, dropout backward, and softmax backward into a single kernel, with a separate optimized dV kernel exploiting GQA
active_paradigms.description: Write a Triton kernel that: (1) for dS computation - loads dO tiles and V tiles (shared across 10 query heads per KV head), computes dP_dropped = dO @ V^T in the kernel, applies dropout mask and scale, then computes softmax backward dS = P*(dP - sum(dP*P)) all in one fused pass without materializing dP; (2) for dV computation - write a separate Triton kernel that accumulates contributions from all 10 query groups into dV, loading P_dropped and dO tiles. Use block sizes tuned for B200 (BLOCK_M=64, BLOCK_N=64, BLOCK_K=128). The key optimization is that V tiles are reused across all 10 query heads in the same KV group, reducing memory bandwidth by 10x for the V loads. Store dS directly in bf16. Use tl.dot for tensor cores.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Memory bandwidth reduction by fusing operations and exploiting GQA structure to reuse V across 10 query heads, eliminating intermediate tensor materialization of dP_dropped [bs,80,sq,skv]
active_paradigms.cautions: Triton kernel correctness is tricky - test against reference carefully. Handle non-power-of-2 sequence lengths with masking. The softmax backward requires accumulating sum(dP*P) which needs a reduction within the kernel. Use float32 accumulators for the matmul and softmax backward. Be careful with the GQA indexing: head_idx // 10 gives the KV head index.

active_paradigms.idea: Pure PyTorch with careful memory layout optimization: avoid torch.compile overhead, use einsum/bmm with optimal tensor layouts and explicit float32 promotion only where needed
active_paradigms.description: Skip torch.compile entirely (it adds JIT overhead on first calls and may not optimize well for variable shapes). Instead: (1) Use contiguous bf16 tensors throughout, only promoting to float32 for the softmax backward numerics. (2) For dP computation, use torch.baddbmm or reshape+bmm exploiting GQA - reshape dO to [bs*8, 10, sq, 128], V to [bs*8, 1, 128, skv], use expand+bmm to get [bs*8, 10, sq, skv]. (3) For softmax backward, keep in float32 but use in-place operations to reduce memory allocation. (4) For dV, use einsum('bgsq,bgsqd->bgsd', P_dropped_grouped, dO_grouped) which PyTorch can optimize to a batched matmul with summation. (5) Use torch.cuda.Stream to potentially overlap dS and dV computations. Avoid .contiguous() calls where possible by ensuring tensors are already in the right layout.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Reduce Python/compilation overhead, minimize memory allocations, use optimal BLAS calls for the specific GQA structure with bs*8 independent matmul batches
active_paradigms.cautions: Without torch.compile, need to manually ensure tensor contiguity for efficient matmul. The expand() for V broadcasting must be followed by contiguous() before bmm or use as_strided carefully. Stream parallelism between dS and dV paths requires careful synchronization. Test that einsum maps to efficient cuBLAS calls rather than slow element-wise ops.

tried_paradigms.idea: Custom Triton kernel fusing dropout backward + softmax backward + both BMMs
tried_paradigms.description: Write a Triton kernel that processes tiles of the attention weight matrix. For dS computation: load tiles of P and dP_dropped, apply dropout mask and scale, compute softmax backward locally. For dV: accumulate contributions from P_dropped^T @ dO across the sq dimension. The key insight is that the [bs, 80, sq, skv] tensors are the bottleneck - reading them multiple times is expensive. A fused kernel reads P, P_dropped, dropout_mask once and computes both dS and partial dV in one pass. Use separate Triton kernels for dS (fused with dropout) and dV (exploiting GQA by having each kernel block handle one KV head, accumulating over 10 query heads). For dV specifically: launch [bs, 8, skv//BLOCK, 128//BLOCK] blocks, each iterating over sq and the 10 groups, accumulating in fp32 registers. For dS: launch [bs, 80, sq, 1] blocks, each computing the row-wise softmax backward.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Memory bandwidth - avoid multiple passes over large tensors, reduce global memory traffic by fusing elementwise ops with matmuls
tried_paradigms.cautions: Need careful handling of non-power-of-2 sequence lengths with masking. The GQA structure means dV kernel should accumulate over 10 groups. Use tl.dot for the matmul tiles. Ensure bf16 inputs with fp32 accumulation. Handle the bs*80 batch dimension carefully for parallelism.

tried_paradigms.idea: Flash-attention-style fused Triton kernel using CUDA graphs + optimized memory layout
tried_paradigms.description: Implement two highly optimized Triton kernels: (1) dS kernel that processes [bs*80, sq, skv] with each program handling one row of sq, loading P and P_dropped tiles along skv, computing dP=dP_dropped*mask/scale, then softmax_bwd = P*(dP - sum(dP*P)) all in registers without materializing dP_dropped globally - instead computing it on-the-fly from dO and V using tl.dot. (2) dV kernel that uses GQA structure: each Triton program handles one (bs, kv_head, skv_block) and accumulates over all 10 query groups and sq positions. Wrap with torch.cuda.make_graphed_callables or use cudagraph_trees via torch.compile with CUDA graphs to eliminate kernel launch overhead. The fused approach for dS avoids writing dP_dropped to HBM entirely - dO @ V^T is computed tile-by-tile inside the kernel and immediately used for softmax backward.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Eliminate HBM writes of intermediate dP_dropped tensor, reduce kernel launch overhead with CUDA graphs, maximize arithmetic intensity
tried_paradigms.cautions: The fully fused dS kernel (computing dO@V^T on-the-fly) requires loading both dO and V tiles which increases register pressure. May need to tune BLOCK_SIZE carefully. For non-square cases (sq != skv), need proper boundary masking. The GQA dV kernel needs to handle the group reduction correctly.
